section.title: The Fall of Tyre
section.sectionRationale: Tyre gloated that Jerusalem's fall would fill her own harbors with the trade Jerusalem once controlled. This oracle answers that greed with a sentence: the LORD Himself will raise up many nations against Tyre, scrape her down to bare rock, and bring her to the Pit, showing that no maritime empire lies outside His sovereign reach.
section.thesis: The LORD who judged Jerusalem for her sin now judges Tyre for gloating over that very judgment and grasping at Jerusalem's ruin for profit, proving that His justice reaches every proud city, however secure it feels behind its walls and its wealth.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Tyre's gloating and the LORD's sentence against her; the specific coming of Nebuchadnezzar's siege; the lament of the princes of the sea over her fall; and Tyre brought down to the Pit among the peoples of old.

units.label: Tyre's gloating and the LORD's sentence

verses.text: In the eleventh month of the twelfth year, on the first day of the month, the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, because Tyre has said of Jerusalem, 'Aha! The gate to the nations is broken; it has swung open to me; now that she lies in ruins I will be filled,'
therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: 'Behold, O Tyre, I am against you, and I will raise up many nations against you, as the sea brings up its waves.
They will destroy the walls of Tyre and demolish her towers. I will scrape the soil from her and make her a {{sela|bare rock}}.
She will become a place to spread nets in the sea, for I have spoken, declares the Lord GOD. She will become plunder for the nations,
and the villages on her mainland will be slain by the sword. Then they will know that I am the LORD.'

units.label: Nebuchadnezzar's siege declared
units.note: king of kings sent against her

verses.text: For this is what the Lord GOD says: 'Behold, I will bring against Tyre from the north Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, king of kings, with horses and chariots, with cavalry and a great company of troops.
He will slaughter the villages of your mainland with the sword; he will set up siege works against you, build a ramp to your walls, and raise his shields against you.
He will direct the blows of his battering rams against your walls and tear down your towers with his axes.
His multitude of horses will cover you in their dust. When he enters your gates as an army entering a breached city, your walls will shake from the noise of cavalry, wagons, and chariots.
The hooves of his horses will trample all your streets. He will slaughter your people with the sword, and your mighty pillars will fall to the ground.
They will plunder your wealth and pillage your merchandise. They will demolish your walls, tear down your beautiful homes, and throw your stones and timber and soil into the water.
So I will silence the sound of your songs, and the music of your lyres will no longer be heard.
I will make you a bare rock, and you will become a place to spread the fishing nets. You will never be rebuilt, for I, the LORD, have spoken, declares the Lord GOD.'

units.label: The princes of the sea lament

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says to Tyre: 'Will not the coastlands quake at the sound of your downfall, when the wounded groan at the slaughter in your midst?

All the princes of the sea will descend from their thrones, remove their robes, and strip off their embroidered garments. Clothed with terror, they will sit on the ground, trembling every moment, appalled over you.

Then they will {{qinah|lament}} for you, saying, 'How you have perished, O city of renown inhabited by seafaring men, she who was powerful on the sea, along with her people, who imposed terror on all peoples!
Now the coastlands tremble on the day of your downfall; the islands in the sea are dismayed by your demise.'
For this is what the Lord GOD says: 'When I make you a desolate city like other deserted cities, and when I raise up the deep against you so that the mighty waters cover you,

then I will bring you down with those who descend to the {{bor|Pit}}, to the people of antiquity. I will make you dwell in the earth below like the ancient ruins, with those who descend to the Pit, so that you will no longer be inhabited or set in splendor in the land of the living.
I will make you an object of horror, and you will be no more. You will be sought, but will never be found,' declares the Lord GOD.”

sela.gloss: A rock or cliff, here stripped bare of soil, buildings, and life. The image is of Tyre's proud, fortified island scraped down to nothing, later fulfilled with striking literalness when Alexander the Great used the rubble of Tyre's abandoned mainland city to build a causeway to her island, scraping the mainland site bare.

qinah.gloss: A funeral dirge or lament, the same form used to mourn a death. The princes of the sea sing a qinah over Tyre as though for a person who has died, underscoring how total and personal her fall would feel to the nations who once depended on her.

bor.gloss: The Pit, a term for the grave or the realm of the dead, often used alongside Sheol. Tyre's descent to the Pit places her among the peoples of antiquity long since dead, a picture of total, final humbling before the living God.

basic.context: Tyre, the great Phoenician sea power built on a fortified island just off the Lebanese coast, responded to Jerusalem's fall with cold satisfaction, expecting to inherit the trade that once flowed through Jerusalem's gate. This oracle answers that greed directly: the LORD will raise up many nations against Tyre, beginning with Nebuchadnezzar, and bring the proud sea city down to a bare rock and finally to the Pit.
basic.hermeneutics: This describes a real, historically anchored judgment fulfilled in two distinct stages: Nebuchadnezzar's actual thirteen year siege of Tyre's mainland settlement, described in verses 7 to 11, and a later, more total devastation of the island fortress itself, fulfilled when Alexander the Great built a causeway from the rubble of the abandoned mainland city in 332 BC, literally scraping the site into the sea as verse 12 describes. The chapter is not a general template for judging cities but a specific, dated prophecy against one real city's specific sin.
basic.originalLanguages: Tyre is reduced to a sela, a bare rock, her proud fortifications and soil scraped away. The princes of the sea raise a qinah, a funeral dirge, over her as though mourning a death, not merely a military defeat. And she is finally brought down to the bor, the Pit, joining the peoples of antiquity in the realm of the dead, a picture of total and final humbling.
basic.typology: Tyre's descent to the Pit, the realm of the dead, foreshadows the biblical pattern of every proud human power eventually humbled before God, a pattern Christ Himself entered and reversed, descending into death yet rising in victory over it, so that all who are His need not fear the Pit's final word.
basic.christ: Tyre's proud self-sufficiency, 'I will be filled' at another's expense, is judged by the God who alone raises up and brings down nations; centuries later, Jesus ministered with grace near Tyre and Sidon, extending faith honoring mercy to a woman from that very region, Matthew 15:21-28, and the early church later found believers already gathered in Tyre, Acts 21:3-6, a living picture of grace reaching the very city once condemned here.
basic.god: God is sovereign over empires and armies alike, raising up 'many nations against you, as the sea brings up its waves' and naming Nebuchadnezzar as His own instrument, 'king of kings', language elsewhere reserved for God's ultimate authority. Yet He is also patient with pride's slow discovery of its own smallness, letting the princes of the sea themselves sing the dirge that proves His word true.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 23 gives an earlier, companion oracle against Tyre focused on her trade and her eventual restored profit being set apart to the LORD. Amos 1:9-10 and Joel 3:4-8 condemn Tyre for trading captured Israelites into slavery. Matthew 15:21-28 shows Jesus extending grace to a woman from Tyre and Sidon's region. Revelation 18 echoes this chapter's maritime merchant imagery in describing Babylon's own commercial fall.
basic.oneStory: Tyre's proud, self-serving gloating over Jerusalem's fall is met by the LORD's sovereign hand, working even through pagan Babylon and later Alexander to bring the sea's own princes low. This anticipates the whole biblical pattern that every proud human power is eventually humbled before God, and that even a city as hostile as Tyre is not beyond the reach of the grace that later gathered disciples within her own walls.

context.literary: The first of three chapters devoted to Tyre, 26 to 28, within the larger collection of nation oracles, chapters 25 to 32; this chapter moves from sentence, to specific military detail, to lament, to final descent, a structure later chapters develop further with an extended lament, chapter 27, and a probing meditation on pride itself, chapter 28.
context.historical: Tyre was built partly on a fortified island a short distance off the Phoenician coast, making her seem nearly unconquerable; Nebuchadnezzar besieged the mainland settlement for thirteen years, roughly 585 to 572 BC, without fully subduing the island stronghold, which finally fell to Alexander the Great in 332 BC after he built a causeway to it using, according to ancient historians, the rubble of the abandoned mainland city, an event matching this oracle's details with remarkable precision.
context.canonical: Opens the extended Tyre sequence within Ezekiel's nation oracles and stands alongside Isaiah 23 as the two major biblical oracles against this city, together forming Scripture's fullest treatment of a maritime trading empire's rise and fall before God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To declare that the LORD Himself, not merely regional politics, will bring down Tyre's pride and profiteering off Jerusalem's fall, using pagan armies as His own instruments, so that Tyre and all who watch will know that He alone is sovereign over the nations.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage describes a specific historical judgment on one ancient city for a specific sin, gloating profiteering; it does not prescribe how to relate to modern trading rivals or geopolitical competitors. Its abiding principle is that no city's wealth or fortification places it beyond God's justice, and that rejoicing over another's calamity invites His response.

debates.question: Does the prophecy that Tyre would 'never be rebuilt' and be 'no more' fail, since settlements at the site of Tyre continued to exist after Ezekiel's time?

views.label: A prophecy fulfilled in two distinct historical stages
views.case: Verses 7 to 11 describe Nebuchadnezzar's specific siege of Tyre's mainland city, which he did indeed conquer after roughly thirteen years, while verses 3 to 6 and 12 to 14, many nations, scraping the soil into the sea, describe a further, later devastation, fulfilled when Alexander the Great used the rubble of that same abandoned mainland city to build a causeway and finally raze the long unconquered island fortress in 332 BC, an extraordinary, multi-stage fulfillment matching the oracle's specific details.

views.label: The absolute language reflects standard ancient conquest idiom, not a claim about every future settlement
views.case: Ancient Near Eastern royal and prophetic oracles regularly used sweeping language, 'never rebuilt', 'will be no more', to describe decisive, humbling military defeat rather than a literal claim that no settlement would ever again exist at that location; the oracle's central point, Tyre's proud maritime empire being shattered, was thoroughly fulfilled even though a much lesser town later occupied part of the site.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm that the oracle's central claim, God's sovereign judgment shattering Tyre's pride and imperial power through pagan armies, was fulfilled with remarkable historical precision; the debate concerns only how literally to press the details of later, lesser habitation.

study.originalLanguages: Tyre, once proud and fortified, is reduced to a sela, a bare rock, scraped clean of soil and splendor. The princes of the sea respond with a qinah, a funeral dirge, mourning her as though she were a person who had died rather than merely a city defeated. And her final destination is the bor, the Pit, joined with the long dead peoples of antiquity, a picture of total and final humbling before the living God.

typology.type: Tyre brought down to the Pit among the peoples of antiquity
typology.fulfillment: Foreshadows the biblical pattern of every proud human power eventually humbled before God, a descent Christ Himself entered in death, yet reversed in resurrection victory over the Pit's final claim, 1 Corinthians 15:54-57.

study.christ: Tyre's self-serving greed, gloating over Jerusalem's ruin to fill her own harbors, is judged by the God who alone raises up and brings down nations. Centuries later, Jesus Himself ministered with grace in the region of Tyre and Sidon, honoring the faith of a woman from that very territory, Matthew 15:21-28, and the early church later found believing disciples already gathered within Tyre's own walls, Acts 21:3-6, a striking picture of grace reaching the very city this oracle condemns.
study.god: God is sovereign over empires: He raises up 'many nations' against Tyre as easily as the sea raises its waves, and calls pagan Nebuchadnezzar His own instrument, 'king of kings', language that elsewhere belongs to God's own supreme authority. Yet He is not merely destructive; He lets the princes of the sea themselves sing the lament that proves His word true, and history itself, through two distinct conquerors centuries apart, bears out the precision of what He spoke.

crossReferences.note: An earlier companion oracle against Tyre, focused on her trade and the promise that her restored profit would one day be set apart to the LORD.
Condemns Tyre for trading whole communities of captives into slavery, a related picture of her moral corruption through trade.
Jesus extends grace to a Canaanite woman from the region of Tyre and Sidon, honoring her persistent faith.
Echoes this chapter's imagery of merchants and kings lamenting a great trading city's sudden fall, applied there to Babylon.

study.oneStory: Tyre's proud gloating over Jerusalem's fall is met by the LORD's sovereign hand, working through pagan Babylon and later Alexander to bring the sea's own princes to lament and the island city itself down to the Pit. This anticipates the whole pattern of Scripture, that every proud human power is eventually humbled before God, while grace, astonishingly, still reaches even a city as hostile as Tyre, where disciples were later found gathered in Christ's name.

quiz.q: Why did the LORD pronounce judgment on Tyre in this chapter?

options.text: Because Tyre gloated over Jerusalem's fall, expecting to profit from the trade her ruin left behind.
options.note: Verse 2 gives Tyre's exact words of gloating self interest as the provoking sin.

options.text: Because Tyre had worshiped idols for centuries.
options.note: Idolatry is not the specific accusation named in this chapter; the stated sin is gloating profiteering over Jerusalem's fall.

options.text: Because Tyre had refused to pay tribute to Egypt.
options.note: Not mentioned in this text at all; the accusation concerns her attitude toward Jerusalem's ruin.

options.text: Because Tyre had broken a trade treaty with Israel generations earlier.
options.note: No treaty violation is cited; the specific sin named is her cold, self interested reaction to Jerusalem's fall.

quiz.q: Who does the LORD specifically name as His instrument to bring against Tyre?

options.text: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon, called 'king of kings' in this oracle.
options.note: Verse 7 names Nebuchadnezzar directly and gives him this striking title.

options.text: Pharaoh king of Egypt.
options.note: Egypt is not named as the instrument against Tyre in this chapter; that role belongs to Nebuchadnezzar.

options.text: The king of Assyria.
options.note: Assyria had already fallen by this period; the named instrument here is Babylon's king.

options.text: An unnamed coalition of Phoenician rivals.
options.note: The text is specific: Nebuchadnezzar of Babylon is named directly as the LORD's chosen instrument.

quiz.q: How do the princes of the sea respond to Tyre's downfall, according to verses 15 to 18?

options.text: They descend from their thrones in terror and raise a funeral lament over her as over the dead.
options.note: Verses 16 to 17 describe exactly this response, trembling and a formal qinah, or lament, for Tyre.

options.text: They celebrate, since Tyre was their commercial rival.
options.note: The text describes mourning and terror, not celebration, among the neighboring princes.

options.text: They immediately send military aid to rebuild Tyre.
options.note: No rescue or rebuilding effort is described; their response is lament and dread, not aid.

options.text: They remain indifferent, unaffected by her fall.
options.note: The text describes strong emotional and physical reactions, trembling, appalled, not indifference.

quiz.q: What does 'bringing Tyre down to the Pit' in verses 19 to 20 picture?

options.text: A total and final humbling, joining Tyre with the long dead peoples of antiquity in the realm of the dead.
options.note: Verse 20 explicitly places Tyre among 'the people of antiquity' who have already descended to the Pit.

options.text: A temporary exile from which Tyre would quickly return to power.
options.note: The imagery describes a final, total humbling, not a brief or reversible exile.

options.text: A literal underground prison built by Babylon for Tyre's leaders.
options.note: The Pit here is a term for the realm of the dead or the grave, not a literal Babylonian prison structure.

options.text: A description only of Tyre's economic decline, with no reference to death or destruction.
options.note: The imagery is explicitly about descent among the dead, a far more total picture than mere economic downturn.

quiz.q: How does history relate to this oracle's specific details about Tyre's destruction?

options.text: Nebuchadnezzar besieged the mainland city for years, and Alexander the Great later used its rubble to build a causeway that finally razed the island fortress, matching the oracle in two stages.
options.note: This two stage historical fulfillment, Babylon then Alexander, is well attested and matches the oracle's specific details, including scraping soil into the sea, verse 12.

options.text: No historical event ever matched any detail of this prophecy.
options.note: Historical sources describe both Nebuchadnezzar's siege and Alexander's causeway built from the old city's rubble, closely matching this oracle.

options.text: Tyre was destroyed in a single, immediate conquest by Nebuchadnezzar alone.
options.note: Nebuchadnezzar's siege did not fully subdue the island fortress; that fell later, to Alexander the Great.

options.text: The prophecy was fulfilled only in a purely symbolic, non historical sense.
options.note: The fulfillment is unusually concrete and historical, not merely symbolic, matching real, datable military events.

questions: Tyre said 'Aha' when Jerusalem fell, hoping to profit from another's ruin. Where have you caught yourself feeling satisfaction, even quietly, at someone else's downfall or misfortune?
Tyre trusted in her island fortress and her wealth to keep her safe from any threat. What fortress, financial, relational, or reputational, are you tempted to trust in more than you trust God?

The LORD calls pagan Nebuchadnezzar His own instrument, 'king of kings,' even though Nebuchadnezzar did not know or serve Him. Where do you see God using circumstances or people outside your control to accomplish His purposes in your life right now?
The princes of the sea mourned Tyre as though for a death, finally seeing what they had refused to see while she stood. What warning are you tempted to ignore until it is too late to act on it?

Grace later reached the very city and region this oracle condemns, in Jesus' ministry near Tyre and Sidon and in the disciples Paul later found there. Who or what feels too far gone or too hostile for God's grace to reach, and how might this chapter's ending in Acts challenge that assumption?

translationNotes.note: Where the BSB source text prints a line break mid verse, poetic formatting has been joined into a single running line for readability, following house style.
Where BSB prints an em dash before 'she who was powerful on the sea,' it has been rendered as a comma per house style.

The title 'king of kings' applied to Nebuchadnezzar is a striking use of royal language elsewhere associated with supreme authority, underscoring how completely the LORD commands even the greatest pagan rulers as His own instruments.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Ezekiel 26:1-21. No verified sermon from the trusted cluster directly expounding this specific passage was found this session; sermon status is none.
